topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright’s departure was confirmed, effective at the end of the semester. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into an indistinct smear. My hands, resting on the cool wooden desk, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I reread the message, searching for some hidden reassurance, but found only the stark finality of administrative news. A tightness constricted my chest, making each breath shallow. I pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. I walked to the window, the afternoon light feeling suddenly too bright, too exposing. Outside, the campus seemed vibrant and indifferent to the seismic shift happening within my academic world.

[story 2]

"So, you're really leaving?" I managed, the question sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. The words caught in my throat, lodging there like grit. I watched Dr. Evans pack up his office, his movements precise and economical. Every rustle of paper, every click of a pen seemed amplified in the sudden silence of the hallway. A strange, hollow sensation settled in my stomach, a cold weight that made my limbs feel sluggish. I tried to muster a polite smile, but it felt brittle, like thin ice ready to crack under any pressure. When he finally turned, his expression was professional, distant, and utterly devoid of the shared history we’d built over the last year.

[story 3]

I spent an hour pacing my small dorm room, the worn carpet absorbing the sound of my restless movements. The silence of the room was oppressive, magnifying the thumping rhythm of my own anxiety. I picked up the thesis draft, the pages suddenly feeling heavy and suffocating in my hands. Why was this happening now? All those late nights fueled by shared intellectual struggle, the tentative breakthroughs we’d celebrated—it all seemed to evaporate with the news. I dropped the manuscript onto the bed, the sound surprisingly loud. I sank onto the edge of the mattress, burying my face in my arms, the tension in my shoulders refusing to release, leaving me feeling utterly exposed and adrift.
